title: Exact nonequilibrium steady states of boundary driven circuit with XYZ gates
authors: Xin Zhang, Tomaz Prosen, Vladislav Popkov
abstract: We obtain the exact many-body density operator of a boundary-driven XXZ quantum circuit via a spatially inhomogeneous matrix product Ansatz. The Ansatz has formally infinite bond-dimension and generalizes authors' previous construction \cite{2025XXZcircuit} for the XXZ interactions. The boundary qubits are coupled to reset quantum channels that project them toward arbitrary pure target states. We find and describe a family of relatively robust separable chiral nonequilibrium steady states (NESS), which are elliptic analogs of spin helices for the circuit, and which   are particularly attractive from an experimental perspective.
summary: The paper provides an exact solution for the steady state of a boundary-driven XYZ quantum circuit. It identifies a new class of robust, chiral, and separable steady states called elliptic spin helices. These states are particularly interesting because they are computationally tractable and potentially easy to implement in experimental settings.
main_problem: Finding the exact many-body density operator for the nonequilibrium steady state (NESS) of a boundary-driven XYZ quantum circuit, generalizing previous XXZ solutions to the fully anisotropic case.
main_result: The authors derive an exact solution using a spatially inhomogeneous matrix product ansatz and discover a family of robust, separable, chiral steady states known as 'elliptic brickwork spin helices'.
method: The study employs a two-replica Matrix Product Ansatz (MPA) with an infinite bond dimension, utilizing the algebraic structure of inhomogeneous Lax operators and Jacobi theta functions.
model_or_system: A 1D brickwork quantum circuit of N+2 qubits with XYZ-type unitary gates, where boundary qubits are subject to reset quantum channels that project them toward arbitrary pure target states.
key_observables: Local magnetization profiles (x, y, z components), one-point correlations at the boundary, and helix indicators (f1, f2) measuring purity and distance to the helix state.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter (eta), Trotter time step (u), boundary reset parameters (alpha_L, alpha_R), and the modulus of the elliptic theta function (tau).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the validity of the proposed matrix product ansatz and relies on the algebraic properties of the Yang-Baxter RLL-type relations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the circuit architecture; Figure 5 and 6 show helix indicators (f1, f2) as functions of anisotropy, identifying the presence of pure helices, descendants with kinks, and different chiralities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the XYZ circuit model, presents the Matrix Product Ansatz solution, describes the discovery of elliptic spin helices, and provides rigorous mathematical proofs in the appendices.
why_interesting: This work provides an exact analytical solution for a non-equilibrium system without U(1) symmetry, identifying robust, experimentally accessible chiral states that could be useful for studying many-body dynamics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. 2-step reset driven XYZ circuit in folded notation. Empty circles represent a local traces while red/blue disks represent arbitrary pure boundary states. The right scheme correspond to an equivalent reduced circuit where squares rep- resent boundary Kraus maps (see details in the main text).

Figure 3. Average local magnetizations profiles for elliptic brickwork helix (48) for real η. Top panel: black, blue, green points connected with full lines correspond to ⟨σx n⟩, ⟨σy n⟩, ⟨σz n⟩ respectively. Bottom panel: Blowup of ⟨σy n⟩for even/odd steps of evolution (50), (51) is shown with full blue and dashed red lines respectively. Parameters: M = 60, u = 0.15i, τ = 0.65i, αL = 0.095 + 0.1i, η = 4/M.

Figure 4. Average local magnetizations profiles for ellip- tic brickwork helix (48) for imaginary η. Black and blue points connected with full lines correspond to ⟨σx n⟩and ⟨σy n⟩, respectively. Dashed lines show the same quantity at odd steps of evolution (51). Parameters: M = 30, u = 0.15, τ = 0.325i, αL = 0.095 + 0.1i, η = 2τ/M.

Figure 5. Helix indicators −f1 (solid blue curve) and f2(η) (solid black curve), given by (54) and (55) respectively and measured in the NESS, plotted versus the rescaled anisotropy for a system of N = 7 sites. Top Panel: u = 0.185i, τ = 0.65i, αL = 0.165 + 0.13i, αR = αL + u. Bottom Panel: u = 0.185, τ = 0.65i, αL = 0, αR = u = 0.185. Zeros of the in- dicators coincide with the pure helix condition (N + 1)η = 0 (mod 2), (red dashed lines), while less pronounced minima (green dashed lines) occur at anisotropies leading to helix with integer number of kinks mk = 1 (helix descendants) (N + 1 −2mk)η = 0, mk = 1.

Figure 6. Helix indicators −f1 (blue curve), f2(±η) (black curve) given by (54), (55), versus the anisotropy, for system with N = 9 sites. Zeros of f1 are marked by black and red points. Parameters: u = 0.185i, τ = 0.65i, αL = 0, αR = αL + u. Top Panel: a subset of zeros of f1 (black points ) coincides with the pure helix condition (N +1)η = 0 (mod 2), and corresponds to helices of type (33) (red dashed lines). Helix descendants with one/two kinks correspond to (N + 1 −2mk)η = 0 (mod 2) with mk = 1, 2, and are marked with green/blue dashed lines. Red points mark location of helices with opposite helicity of the form (37), appearing at (N+1)η = 1 (mod 2), see text for more explanations....
